sections.summary: This section establishes a regulatory framework for financial institutions in Mauritius to manage outsourcing risks. It defines outsourcing as engaging third-party service providers for ongoing activities that would normally be handled internally, emphasizing the need for a risk management framework to mitigate risks such as financial instability and regulatory gaps. The framework requires institutions to classify activities into material, non-material, and core categories, with material activities needing Bank authorization. Financial institutions must develop comprehensive policies, conduct due diligence, and ensure board oversight to address risks associated with outsourcing. Financial institutions must establish formal written contracts for outsourcing activities, clearly defining rights and responsibilities. Contracts must not impede the Bank's supervisory powers, allow on-site inspections, and include clauses for prior Bank approval of subcontracting material activities. The Bank may require modifications or termination of arrangements for customer or stakeholder protection. [extraction failed: model output unparseable]
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that financial institutions implement robust risk management for outsourcing, requiring classification of activities, due diligence on service providers, and contractual safeguards to ensure regulatory compliance and operational stability. It directly impacts DTIAs by necessitating assessments of cross-border outsourcing risks and service provider vetting. Outsourcing contracts must include specific safeguards for regulatory oversight, on-site access, and Bank approval for material activities to ensure compliance with cross-border data and operational risks.

requirements.text: Prior to outsourcing any activity, a financial institution should establish a comprehensive policy on outsourcing that guides assessment of whether and how an activity should be outsourced.

The board of directors and senior management must ensure an effective risk management system on outsourcing, including approving outsourcing policies, assessing strategies, and reviewing material outsourcing activities annually.

Financial institutions must carry out stringent due diligence in selecting service providers, including assessing experience, reputation, financial soundness, internal controls, and business continuity management.
Outsourcing arrangements must be governed by formal written contracts that clearly define rights and responsibilities and allow for on-site inspections by the Bank.
Outsourcing arrangements between financial institutions and service providers should be governed by formal and comprehensive written contracts.
Contracts should clearly spell out the rights and responsibilities of each party, taking into consideration the specificities and materiality of the outsourcing activities.
The agreement should not consist of clauses that would hinder the Bank from exercising its supervisory powers.
The Bank should have the same right of access to information with the service provider as it has with the financial institutions having undertaken the outsourcing.
The contract should explicitly allow for on-site visits and unhindered inspections of the outsourced activities by the financial institutions and the Bank.
The cost of on-site examinations shall be borne by financial institutions.

The agreement should consist of a clause for seeking the prior approval of the Bank in the event of sub-contracting of material activities which have been outsourced by a financial institution to any other entity.

sections.source_quote: This Guideline is issued under the authority of section 50 of the Bank of Mauritius Act 2004 and section 100 of the Banking Act 2004.

sections.summary: Financial institutions must have their external auditors review and attest the adequacy of their outsourcing policies and processes. Auditors must immediately report any material weaknesses or irregularities to the Bank of Mauritius that could impact the financial institution's well-being or create operational risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires external audit verification of outsourcing compliance, which is critical for DTIAs to ensure robust oversight of third-party service providers and timely risk reporting to regulators.

requirements.text: The external auditor should review and attest the adequacy of the policies and processes put in place by financial institutions for outsourcing activities.

They should immediately inform the Bank of any material weaknesses or irregularities that, in their opinion, might affect the well-being of the financial institution or have additional operational risk implications.

sections.source_quote: 5.1 The external auditor should review and attest the adequacy of the policies and processes put in place by financial institutions for outsourcing activities. They should immediately inform the Bank of any material weaknesses or irregularities that, in their opinion, might affect the well-being of the financial institution or have additional operational risk implications.

sections.summary: This section states that the Guideline on Outsourcing by Financial Institutions supersedes section 12 of the Guideline on Internet Banking, which previously addressed outsourcing of banks' Internet banking activities.

requirements.text: In February 2001, the Bank issued to all banks a guideline entitled ‘Guideline on Internet Banking'. The guideline deals with issues relating to Internet banking and section 12 thereof deals exclusively with outsourcing of banks' Internet banking activity.
This Guideline on Outsourcing by Financial Institutions covers broadly the outsourcing-related issues. As such, it supersedes section 12 of the Guideline on Internet Banking.

sections.source_quote: 6.1 In February 2001, the Bank issued to all banks a guideline entitled ‘Guideline on Internet Banking'. The guideline deals with issues relating to Internet banking and section 12 thereof deals exclusively with outsourcing of banks' Internet banking activity. This Guideline on Outsourcing by Financial Institutions covers broadly the outsourcing-related issues. As such, it supersedes section 12 of the Guideline on Internet Banking.

sections.summary: Financial institutions must seek prior authorization from the Bank of Mauritius before entering into material outsourcing arrangements. They must assess existing outsourcing activities against this Guideline, report compliance status within four months, and immediately notify the Bank of any adverse developments affecting their businesses.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates pre-authorization and timely reporting for material outsourcing, which is essential for DTIAs to evaluate regulatory alignment and risk mitigation in cross-border outsourcing scenarios.

requirements.text: This Guideline is applicable to all financial institutions falling under the regulatory purview of the Bank.

Financial institutions should conduct an assessment of all their existing outsourcing arrangements against this Guideline. Where the outsourcing is considered material, financial institutions should inform the Bank in writing as to the level of compliance with the Guideline and report weaknesses, if any. They should also submit a plan and timeframe on how such weaknesses would be rectified. This should be done within 4 months from the effective date of this Guideline.
Financial institutions should inform the Bank immediately, of any adverse development arising from any outsourcing arrangement that could significantly affect their businesses.

sections.source_quote: 7.1 This Guideline is applicable to all financial institutions falling under the regulatory purview of the Bank. It needs to be emphasized, as mentioned in section 4.1.3, that financial institutions should seek prior authorization of the Bank before entering into material outsourcing.
